title: Fractions
objective: Students will explore fractions concepts including Equivalent fractions (age 9+), Decimals for Tenths & Hundredths, Converting tenths to hundredths and more.

steps.content: Welcome to an exciting lesson about Equivalent fractions (age 9+)!
Explain why a fraction a/b is equivalent to (n×a)/(n×b) using visual models; use this principle to recognise and generate equivalent fractions, including tenths and hundredths
Let me show you something cool. Use a fraction strip to show 2/3 = 4/6 = 6/9
If you needs to make 2/3 look different without changing its value, can they multiply the top and bottom by the same number and explain why the fraction stays the same?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Equivalent fractions (age 9+). Use a fraction strip to show 2/3 = 4/6 = 6/9 Explain that multiplying numerator and denominator by the same number gives an equivalent fraction because the size of the whole is unchanged
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Decimals for Tenths & Hundredths!
Use decimal notation for fractions with denominators 10 or 100; read and write decimal numbers as fractions (e.g. 0.62 = 62/100, 0.71 = 71/100)
Time to try this out: Rewrite 0.62 as 62/100
If you sees a price tag showing £0.65, can they rewrite it as a fraction — and go the other way, turning 47/100 into a decimal?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Decimals for Tenths & Hundredths. Rewrite 0.62 as 62/100 Write 3/10 as 0.3 and locate on a number line
Today we're going to learn about Converting tenths to hundredths. Get ready for an adventure!
Express a fraction with denominator 10 as an equivalent fraction with denominator 100 and use this to add fractions with denominators 10 and 100 (e.g. 3/10 + 4/100 = 34/100)
Let's practice together! Rewrite 7/10 as 70/100
If you needs to add 3/10 and 17/100, can they rewrite 3/10 as hundredths first — and then add the two fractions together?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Converting tenths to hundredths: Rewrite 7/10 as 70/100 Calculate 3/10 + 4/100 = 34/100
Have you ever thought about Tenths (age 9+)? Let's find out together!
Recognise and use thousandths; relate them to tenths, hundredths, and their decimal equivalents (e.g. 1/1000 = 0.001, 35/1000 = 0.035)
Let's practice together! Write 0.025 as 25/1000
If you sees the decimal 0.003, can they write it as a fraction and tell you roughly where it sits on a number line between 0 and 0.01?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Tenths (age 9+): Write 0.025 as 25/1000 Explain that 1 tenth = 100 thousandths
Today we're going to learn about Understanding fractions (age 9+). Get ready for an adventure!
Understand a fraction a/b with a > 1 as a sum of fractions 1/b (e.g. 3/5 = 1/5 + 1/5 + 1/5)
Time to try this out: Express 5/8 as a sum of five copies of 1/8
If you ask you to show 4/5 as a sum of smaller equal fractions, can they write it out as 1/5 + 1/5 + 1/5 + 1/5?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Understanding fractions (age 9+): Express 5/8 as a sum of five copies of 1/8 Show on a number line how 4/3 is built by iterating 1/3 four times
Have you ever thought about Comparing Decimals? Let's find out together!
Compare two decimals to hundredths (or up to three decimal places) by reasoning about size using place-value understanding; record with >, =, <
Time to try this out: Compare 0.45 and 0.405 and explain which is greater
If you compares 0.45 and 0.54, can they use place value to explain which is bigger — without reaching for a calculator?
Great job! Today we learned about Comparing Decimals. Compare 0.45 and 0.405 and explain which is greater Order 3.142, 3.14, 3.2 from smallest to largest
Today we're going to learn about Multiplying fractions. Get ready for an adventure!
Understand a/b as a multiple of 1/b; multiply proper fractions and mixed numbers by whole numbers, supported by visual models (e.g. 3 × 2/5 = 6/5 = 1 1/5)
Let me show you something cool. Calculate 4 × 3/8 using repeated addition or the rule n × a/b = (n×a)/b
If a recipe uses 2/3 of a cup of butter and you wants to make it three times over, can they work out how much butter they need in total?
Great job! Today we learned about Multiplying fractions. Calculate 4 × 3/8 using repeated addition or the rule n × a/b = (n×a)/b Multiply 2 1/3 × 3 and express as a mixed number
Let's explore Percentage and decimal equivalents! Have you ever wondered about this?
Solve problems requiring knowledge of percentage and decimal equivalents of 1/2, 1/4, 1/5, 2/5, 4/5 and fractions with denominators that are multiples of 10 or 25
Let me show you something cool. State that 1/5 = 20% = 0.2 and use this to find 20% of 60
If you knows that 1/4 = 25% = 0.25, can they use that to work out 25% of £80 — and also find 4/5 of 60 sweets?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Percentage and decimal equivalents. State that 1/5 = 20% = 0.2 and use this to find 20% of 60 Convert 3/4 to 75% and to 0.75
Let's explore Understanding Percentages! Have you ever wondered about this?
Understand the per cent symbol (%); know that per cent means ‘number of parts per hundred’; write percentages as a fraction with denominator 100 and as a decimal
Let's practice together! Write 35% as 35/100 and as 0.35
If a shop advertises 50% off, can you explain that this means half price — and write 50% as both a fraction and a decimal?
Great job! Today we learned about Understanding Percentages. Write 35% as 35/100 and as 0.35 Shade 40% of a 10×10 grid and write the fraction 40/100
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Adding fractions (different denominators)!
Add and subtract fractions with denominators that are multiples of the same number by finding a common denominator
Let's practice together! Calculate 1/3 + 1/6 by converting to sixths: 2/6 + 1/6 = 3/6 = 1/2
If you needs to add 1/3 and 1/6, can they find a common denominator and work out the total — showing their working?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Adding fractions (different denominators). Calculate 1/3 + 1/6 by converting to sixths: 2/6 + 1/6 = 3/6 = 1/2 Calculate 3/4 − 1/8 by converting to eighths
Today we're going to learn about Comparing fractions (age 9+). Get ready for an adventure!
Compare and order fractions with different numerators and denominators by creating common denominators/numerators or comparing to a benchmark such as 1/2; justify conclusions with visual models
Let me show you something cool. Compare 3/8 and 5/12 by finding a common denominator of 24
If you has eaten 2/3 of their lunch and a friend has eaten 3/4 of theirs, can they work out who's eaten more — by finding a common way to compare the fractions?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Comparing fractions (age 9+): Compare 3/8 and 5/12 by finding a common denominator of 24 Order 2/3, 3/5, and 7/10 from smallest to largest
Have you ever thought about Fraction Addition Concepts? Let's find out together!
Understand addition and subtraction of fractions as joining and separating parts; decompose a fraction into a sum of fractions with the same denominator in more than one way
Time to try this out: Show that 3/8 = 1/8 + 1/8 + 1/8 and also 3/8 = 1/8 + 2/8
If you has 7/8 of a chocolate bar and gives away 3/8, can they work out what fraction is left — and then show two different ways to split 7/8 into two parts?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Fraction Addition Concepts. Show that 3/8 = 1/8 + 1/8 + 1/8 and also 3/8 = 1/8 + 2/8 Write two different decompositions of 5/6
Let's discover the amazing world of Decimals to three places!
Solve problems involving numbers with up to three decimal places
Let's practice together! Calculate the total of three measurements: 1.234 m + 0.567 m + 2.199 m
If you is filling a tank that holds 5.000 litres and has poured in 2.375 litres, can they work out how much more is needed to fill it?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Decimals to three places: Calculate the total of three measurements: 1.234 m + 0.567 m + 2.199 m A bottle holds 1.5 litres; 0.375 l is poured out — how much remains?
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Mixed numbers and improper fractions!
Recognise mixed numbers and improper fractions; convert from one form to the other (e.g. 2/5 + 4/5 = 6/5 = 1 1/5)
Let's practice together! Convert 11/4 to 2 3/4
If you sees the fraction 9/4, can they convert it to a mixed number — and then turn 2 and 3/5 back into an improper fraction?
Great job! Today we learned about Mixed numbers and improper fractions. Convert 11/4 to 2 3/4 Convert 3 2/5 to 17/5
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Adding and subtracting mixed numbers!
Add and subtract mixed numbers with like denominators, including by converting to improper fractions or using properties of operations
Let's practice together! Calculate 2 3/5 + 1 4/5 and express as a mixed number
If a recipe calls for 1 and 2/5 cups of oats and another batch needs 2 and 3/5 cups, can you add them together to get the total — including converting when the fifths add up past a whole?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Adding and subtracting mixed numbers: Calculate 2 3/5 + 1 4/5 and express as a mixed number Subtract 3 1/4 from 5 3/4
Today we're going to learn about Addition and subtraction word problems. Get ready for an adventure!
Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions with like denominators, using visual models and equations
Let me show you something cool. A jug contains 3/4 litre of juice; 2/4 litre is poured out — how much remains?
If you reads that one section of a nature trail is 3/8 km and another is 5/8 km, can they work out the total distance without a calculator?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Addition and subtraction word problems. A jug contains 3/4 litre of juice; 2/4 litre is poured out — how much remains? Two pieces of ribbon are 2 3/8 and 1 5/8 inches — what is their total length?
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Decimal place value (age 9+)!
Round decimals with two decimal places to the nearest whole number and to one decimal place
Let me show you something cool. Round 3.47 to the nearest whole number (3) and to 1 d.p. (3.5)
If you measures a length as 4.67 m, can they round it to the nearest whole metre — and also to the nearest tenth of a metre?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Decimal place value (age 9+). Round 3.47 to the nearest whole number (3) and to 1 d.p. (3.5) Round 12.95 to 1 d.p. (13.0) and explain the boundary case
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Fractions of a whole (age 9+)!
Solve word problems involving multiplication of a fraction by a whole number using visual models and equations
Let's practice together! Each person eats 3/8 of a pizza and there are 5 people — how many pizzas are needed?
If each section of a garden uses 3/4 of a bag of compost and there are 4 sections, can you work out how many bags are needed in total?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Fractions of a whole (age 9+). Each person eats 3/8 of a pizza and there are 5 people — how many pizzas are needed? A ribbon is cut into pieces of 2/3 metre; how long are 4 pieces altogether?
